steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The passage claims Aristophanes satirizes sophists as teaching students to be persuasive without regard for what is morally good. A valid quotation must show a character actively seeking — or a sophist actively providing — instruction in a technique specifically because it is persuasively effective despite being morally wrong.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — ‘Strepsiades encourages his son to learn to be a sophist, saying, “Reform your habits as quickly as possible, and go and learn what I advise.”’: A general push to study — no mention of persuasion, no mention of morality. The amoral dimension is entirely absent. ✗
  • Choice B — ‘Socrates, a sophist, says that he studies astronomy while in a basket hanging a few feet off the ground because “I should not have rightly discovered things celestial if I had not suspended the intellect, and mixed the thought in a subtle form with its kindred air.”’: Shows an eccentric teaching method, not instruction in morally indifferent persuasion. ✗
  • Choice C — ‘Socrates, a sophist, says of a new customer, “I have not seen any man so boorish, nor so impracticable, nor so stupid, nor so forgetful; who, while learning some little petty quibbles, forgets them before he has learned them.”’: Comments on a student's incompetence. Illustrates the sophist's impatience but says nothing about persuasion or morality. ✗
  • Choice D — ‘Strepsiades hires a sophist to teach his son and says to the sophist, “But see that he learns those two [styles of argument]; the better, whatever it may be; and the worse, which, by maintaining what is unjust, overturns the better. If not both, at any rate the unjust one by all means.”’: Strepsiades explicitly requests the "unjust" argument — the one that "overturns the better" — and insists on it above all else. He wants the technique precisely because it wins, even though it is morally wrong. This is the exact split between effectiveness and morality the claim describes. ✓
That confirms D is correct.
